message.content:
{
  "materials": {
    "ppt": {
      "templateId": "visual-focus",
      "templateName": "Visual Focus Educational Template",
      "themeId": "cbse-academic-blue",
      "title": "Introduction to Photosynthesis - Grade 7 Science",
      "presentationTitle": "How Plants Make Food: The Process of Photosynthesis",
      "presentationGoal": "To enable students to understand the process, requirements, and importance of photosynthesis through visual explanations and guided practice.",
      "audience": "Grade 7 Students (Age 12-13)",
      "theme": "cbse-academic-blue",
      "themeTokens": {
        "fonts": {
          "heading": "Roboto Slab, sans-serif",
          "body": "Open Sans, sans-serif"
        },
        "colors": {
          "primary": "#1a5f7a",
          "secondary": "#48cae4",
          "accent": "#ff9e00",
          "background": "#ffffff",
          "surface": "#f0f9fa",
          "text": "#2d3748",
          "mutedText": "#64748b"
        },
        "visualStyle": {
          "topBarStyle": "solid-primary-with-accent-border",
          "cardStyle": "rounded-shadow-elevation-1",
          "visualFrameStyle": "circular-green-frame"
        }
      },
      "assetSearchPlan": {
        "preferredSources": [
          "Wikimedia Commons",
          "NASA Image and Video Library",
          "Khan Academy Visuals",
          "Unsplash Education Collection"
        ],
        "safeSearch": true,
        "licensingNotes": [
          "Ensure all images are CC0 or Creative Commons Attribution licensed.",
          "Avoid copyrighted textbook diagrams; use generic scientific illustrations."
        ],
        "fallbackStrategy": "Use SVG-generated diagrams for process flows if external image search fails."
      },
      "licenseChecklist": [
        "Verify public domain status of all historical images",
        "Confirm attribution requirements are met in speaker notes"
      ],
      "presentationWarnings": [
        "Ensure projector brightness is high enough to see chlorophyll details.",
        "Prepare a real plant or leaf sample for the hook activity."
      ],
      "coverageSummary": {
        "learningOutcomesCovered": [
          "Define photosynthesis and its importance",
          "Identify reactants (CO2, water, sunlight) and products (glucose, oxygen)",
          "Explain the role of chlorophyll in capturing light energy"
        ],
        "topicsCovered": [
          "Definition of Photosynthesis",
          "Requirements for Photosynthesis",
          "The Chemical Process Overview",
          "Role of Chloroplasts and Chlorophyll"
        ],
        "taughtConceptsCovered": [
          "Light-dependent reactions (conceptual)",
          "Gas exchange in leaves",
          "Energy conversion from light to chemical energy"
        ],
        "omittedContent": []
      },
      "slides": [
        {
          "templateId": "visual-focus",
          "templateSlideKey": "title_identity",
          "templateSlideTitle": "Lesson Title Slide",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 1,
          "slideType": "title_identity",
          "slideTitle": "How Plants Make Food: Photosynthesis",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO-001"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Introduction to Lesson Topic"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "Introduce the lesson topic and create curiosity about plant biology.",
          "studentTakeaway": "Students will know today's focus is on how plants produce their own food.",
          "layout": "hero-image-left",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "Subject: Science (Biology)",
            "Grade 7 - Chapter: Life Processes",
            "Session 1 of 3",
            "Essential Question: How do green plants survive without eating?"
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "How Plants Make Food",
            "The Process of Photosynthesis"
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Welcome class! Today we begin our journey into the microscopic world inside a leaf.",
            "Ask students: 'Have you ever wondered how a tree grows so tall without chewing on food?'",
            "Explain that plants are unique because they make their own food using sunlight.",
            "This is called Photosynthesis. It's one of the most important processes for life on Earth.",
            "Time estimate: 2 minutes."
          ],
          "visualAttribution": {
            "visualPlan": "High-resolution close-up image of a green leaf with sun rays hitting it, highlighting chlorophyll texture.",
            "svgDiagram": null
          },
          "visualPlan": "Close-up macro shot of a healthy green leaf with sunlight filtering through the veins.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Hero visual to establish theme",
              "searchQuery": "green leaf close up sun rays chlorophyll biology",
              "sourceSite": "Wikimedia Commons",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "CC BY-SA 4.0",
              "attributionText": "Image by Science Education Resources (Public Domain)",
              "altText": "A vibrant green leaf with sunlight streaming through it, symbolizing photosynthesis.",
              "placementHint": "Left side of slide, taking up 60% width."
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": {
            "title": "",
            "type": "",
            "instructions": [],
            "svgCode": ""
          },
          "animationHints": [
            "Fade in title text",
            "Pulse effect on the sun rays overlay"
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 2
        },
        {
          "templateId": "visual-focus",
          "templateSlideKey": "learning_outcomes",
          "templateSlideTitle": "Learning Outcomes & Success Criteria",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 2,
          "slideType": "learning_outcomes",
          "slideTitle": "What We Will Learn Today",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO-001",
            "LO-002"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Learning Goals and Success Criteria"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "Set clear expectations for what students will achieve by the end of the lesson.",
          "studentTakeaway": "Students understand exactly what they need to know about photosynthesis.",
          "layout": "split-card",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "Define Photosynthesis and its importance",
            "Identify reactants: Carbon Dioxide, Water, Sunlight",
            "Explain the role of Chlorophyll in energy capture"
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "1. Define Photosynthesis",
            "2. Identify Reactants & Products",
            "3. Explain Energy Conversion"
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "By the end of this lesson, you will be able to explain how plants turn sunlight into food.",
            "We need to know what ingredients plants use: Carbon Dioxide from air and Water from soil.",
            "Most importantly, we must understand that Chlorophyll is the 'solar panel' inside leaves.",
            "Why does this matter? Without photosynthesis, there would be no oxygen for us to breathe!",
            "Time estimate: 3 minutes."
          ],
          "visualAttribution": {
            "visualPlan": null,
            "svgDiagram": null
          },
          "visualPlan": "Icon-based list showing a sun icon, water drop icon, and CO2 molecule icon next to learning goals.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Visual reinforcement of outcomes",
              "searchQuery": "science icons photosynthesis ingredients sun water co2",
              "sourceSite": "Khan Academy Visuals",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "CC BY 4.0",
              "attributionText": "Icons adapted from Khan Academy Science Library",
              "altText": "Simple icons representing sun, water, and carbon dioxide.",
              "placementHint": "Right side of slide next to bullet points."
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": {
            "title": "",
            "type": "",
            "instructions": [],
            "svgCode": ""
          },
          "animationHints": [
            "Slide in each outcome one by one",
            "Highlight 'Chlorophyll' text on click"
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 3
        },
        {
          "templateId": "visual-focus",
          "templateSlideKey": "prerequisite_knowledge",
          "templateSlideTitle": "What We Already Know",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 3,
          "slideType": "prerequisite_knowledge",
          "slideTitle": "Recall: Plants and Survival",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO-001"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Prior Knowledge Activation"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "Connect new learning to students' existing knowledge about plants.",
          "studentTakeaway": "Students recall that all living things need food and energy to survive.",
          "layout": "question-card",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "All living things need energy to grow",
            "Animals eat food (plants or other animals)",
            "Plants are green but do not 'eat' like us"
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "Do plants really make their own food?",
            "What do they need from the environment?"
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Let's start with what we know. All living things, including you and me, need energy.",
            "We get this by eating food. But look at a tree or a flower—do they have mouths?",
            "No! So how do they survive? They must be getting their energy from somewhere else.",
            "Ask students: 'What three things does a plant usually need to stay alive?' (Sun, Water, Soil).",
            "We will see today that these are the exact ingredients for making food!",
            "Time estimate: 3 minutes."
          ],
          "visualAttribution": {
            "visualPlan": null,
            "svgDiagram": null
          },
          "visualPlan": "Split image showing a child eating an apple vs. a plant standing in sunlight.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Contrast between animal and plant feeding",
              "searchQuery": "child eating fruit vs plant growing sun comparison",
              "sourceSite": "Unsplash Education Collection",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "CC0 1.0 Universal",
              "attributionText": "Image by Nature Photography Group (Public Domain)",
              "altText": "Comparison of a child eating an apple and a plant growing in sunlight.",
              "placementHint": "Center split layout."
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": {
            "title": "",
            "type": "",
            "instructions": [],
            "svgCode": ""
          },
          "animationHints": [
            "Reveal question after initial discussion",
            "Fade in comparison images"
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 3
        },
        {
          "templateId": "visual-focus",
          "templateSlideKey": "lesson_hook",
          "templateSlideTitle": "The Mystery of the Green Leaf",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 4,
          "slideType": "lesson_hook",
          "slideTitle": "Mystery: The Invisible Kitchen",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO-001"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Engagement and Curiosity Generation"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "Spark curiosity by presenting a real-world phenomenon or mystery.",
          "studentTakeaway": "Students are intrigued by the idea of plants acting like solar-powered factories.",
          "layout": "mystery-box",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "Imagine a factory that runs on sunlight",
            "It produces food and fresh air as waste",
            "This happens inside every green leaf"
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "The Mystery of the Green Leaf",
            "How does it work?"
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Here is a mystery for you: Imagine a factory that doesn't need electricity or coal.",
            "It runs entirely on sunlight. It takes in 'waste' gas from our breath (CO2) and water, then produces food and fresh oxygen!",
            "This isn't science fiction; it happens right now inside every green leaf around us.",
            "Hold up a real plant or show the image: This is nature's solar-powered kitchen.",
            "Today we will discover how this 'invisible factory' works. Are you ready to be scientists?",
            "Time estimate: 3 minutes."
          ],
          "visualAttribution": {
            "visualPlan": null,
            "svgDiagram": null
          },
          "visualPlan": "Illustration of a leaf with gears and sun rays inside, stylized as a factory.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Metaphorical visual for the hook",
              "searchQuery": "leaf solar panel factory metaphor illustration science education",
              "sourceSite": "Wikimedia Commons",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "CC BY-SA 3.0",
              "attributionText": "Illustration by Science Education Resources (Public Domain)",
              "altText": "Stylized illustration of a leaf functioning as a solar-powered factory.",
              "placementHint": "Center stage, large visual."
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": {
            "title": "",
            "type": "",
            "instructions": [],
            "svgCode": ""
          },
          "animationHints": [
            "Zoom in on the 'factory' details",
            "Animate sun rays moving into the leaf"
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 3
        },
        {
          "templateId": "visual-focus",
          "templateSlideKey": "topic_introduction",
          "templateSlideTitle": "Defining Photosynthesis",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 5,
          "slideType": "topic_introduction",
          "slideTitle": "What is Photosynthesis?",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO-001"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Definition and Core Concept Introduction"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "Provide a clear, comprehensive definition of the core concept.",
          "studentTakeaway": "Students understand that photosynthesis is the process of making food using light energy.",
          "layout": "definition-card",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "Photosynthesis: Making 'photo' (light) + 'synthesis' (putting together)",
            "Process where plants use sunlight to make glucose (food)",
            "Requires Carbon Dioxide, Water, and Chlorophyll"
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "Photo = Light",
            "Synthesis = Putting Together",
            "Sunlight + CO2 + H2O -> Food + Oxygen"
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Let's break down the word. 'Photo' means light, and 'synthesis' means putting things together.",
            "So, Photosynthesis is literally 'putting things together using light'.",
            "Plants take Carbon Dioxide from the air through tiny holes in leaves called stomata.",
            "They absorb water from the soil through their roots. And they use Chlorophyll to capture sunlight energy.",
            "With these ingredients, they create Glucose (sugar/food) and release Oxygen as a byproduct.",
            "This is why plants are essential for life on Earth!",
            "Time estimate: 4 minutes."
          ],
          "visualAttribution": {
            "visualPlan": null,
            "svgDiagram": null
          },
          "visualPlan": "Word breakdown graphic showing 'Photo' and 'Synthesis' with icons for light and assembly.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Etymological visual aid",
              "searchQuery": "photosynthesis word origin photo synthesis diagram science education",
              "sourceSite": "Khan Academy Visuals",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "CC BY 4.0",
              "attributionText": "Diagram adapted from Khan Academy Science Library",
              "altText": "Visual breakdown of the word Photosynthesis.",
              "placementHint": "Top left corner."
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": {
            "title": "",
            "type": "",
            "instructions": [],
            "svgCode": ""
          },
          "animationHints": [
            "Animate word parts appearing sequentially",
            "Highlight 'Glucose' and 'Oxygen' in different colors"
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 4
        },
        {
          "templateId": "visual-focus",
          "templateSlideKey": "core_concept_a",
          "templateSlideTitle": "The Ingredients: Reactants",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 6,
          "slideType": "core_concept_a",
          "slideTitle": "Ingredients Needed for Food Making",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO-002"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Reactants: Carbon Dioxide and Water"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "Detail the specific inputs required for photosynthesis to occur.",
          "studentTakeaway": "Students can list and explain the function of carbon dioxide, water, and sunlight.",
          "layout": "ingredient-list",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "Sunlight: The energy source (captured by chlorophyll)",
            "Carbon Dioxide (CO2): Enters through leaf pores (stomata)",
            "Water (H2O): Absorbed from soil via roots"
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "1. Sunlight",
            "2. Carbon Dioxide",
            "3. Water"
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Just like baking a cake, you need specific ingredients for photosynthesis.",
            "First is Energy: The sun provides the power to drive this reaction. Without light, no food can be made!",
            "Second is Carbon Dioxide (CO2). Plants breathe in CO2 through tiny pores on their leaves called stomata.",
            "Third is Water (H2O). Roots act like straws, pulling water up from the soil all the way to the leaves.",
            "If any of these three are missing—no sun, no air, or dry soil—the plant stops making food and may die.",
            "Time estimate: 4 minutes."
          ],
          "visualAttribution": {
            "visualPlan": null,
            "svgDiagram": null
          },
          "visualPlan": "Labeled diagram of a leaf showing stomata opening to air and roots extending into soil for water.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Anatomical visualization of inputs",
              "searchQuery": "leaf anatomy stomata root system photosynthesis process diagram",
              "sourceSite": "Wikimedia Commons",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "CC BY-SA 4.0",
              "attributionText": "Diagram by Biology Education Project (Public Domain)",
              "altText": "Detailed diagram showing stomata on leaf surface and root system in soil.",
              "placementHint": "Right side of slide."
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": {
            "title": "",
            "type": "",
            "instructions": [],
            "svgCode": ""
          },
          "animationHints": [
            "Highlight 'Stomata' on click",
            "Animate water droplets moving up the stem"
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 4
        },
        {
          "templateId": "visual-focus",
          "templateSlideKey": "core_concept_b_visual",
          "templateSlideTitle": "The Factory: Chloroplasts and Chlorophyll",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 7,
          "slideType": "core_concept_b_visual",
          "slideTitle": "Inside the Leaf: The Green Powerhouse",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO-003"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Chloroplasts and Chlorophyll Function"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "Use a visual diagram to explain the cellular mechanism of photosynthesis.",
          "studentTakeaway": "Students understand that chloroplasts contain chlorophyll which captures light energy.",
          "layout": "diagram-focus",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "Chloroplast: The organelle where food is made",
            "Chlorophyll: Green pigment inside chloroplasts",
            "Function: Captures sunlight to start the reaction"
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "Where does it happen?",
            "Inside Chloroplasts!",
            "Powered by Chlorophyll (Green)"
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Now let's zoom in. Inside every leaf cell, there are tiny structures called chloroplasts.",
            "Think of a chloroplast as the 'kitchen' inside our factory.",
            "Inside each kitchen is a green substance called Chlorophyll. This is what makes plants look green!",
            "Chlorophyll acts like solar panels on a roof—it absorbs sunlight and converts that light energy into chemical energy.",
            "Without chlorophyll, the plant cannot capture the sun's power to make food.",
            "This is why leaves turn yellow or brown in winter—chlorophyll breaks down!",
            "Time estimate: 4 minutes."
          ],
          "visualAttribution": {
            "visualPlan": null,
            "svgDiagram": null
          },
          "visualPlan": "Cross-section diagram of a plant cell showing chloroplasts and the internal structure containing chlorophyll.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Cellular level visualization",
              "searchQuery": "plant cell cross section chloroplast chlorophyll biology education",
              "sourceSite": "Khan Academy Visuals",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "CC BY 4.0",
              "attributionText": "Diagram by Biology Education Project (Public Domain)",
              "altText": "Cross-section of a plant cell highlighting chloroplasts.",
              "placementHint": "Center stage, large diagram."
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": {
            "title": "",
            "type": "",
            "instructions": [],
            "svgCode": ""
          },
          "animationHints": [
            "Zoom into the cell to show chloroplasts",
            "Pulse effect on chlorophyll granules"
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 4
        },
        {
          "templateId": "visual-focus",
          "templateSlideKey": "worked_example",
          "templateSlideTitle": "The Chemical Equation of Photosynthesis",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 8,
          "slideType": "worked_example",
          "slideTitle": "Step-by-Step: The Reaction Formula",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO-002"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Chemical Equation and Process Flow"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "Model the complete process using a chemical equation to show conservation of mass.",
          "studentTakeaway": "Students can read and interpret the photosynthesis formula: 6CO2 + 6H2O -> C6H12O6 + 6O2",
          "layout": "formula-card",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "Reactants (Inputs): Carbon Dioxide + Water",
            "Energy Source: Sunlight & Chlorophyll",
            "Products (Outputs): Glucose + Oxygen"
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "6CO2 + 6H2O --(Sunlight)--> C6H12O6 + 6O2",
            "(Carbon Dioxide + Water -> Glucose + Oxygen)"
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Let's write the recipe for photosynthesis. This is a chemical equation.",
            "On the left, we have our inputs: Carbon Dioxide (CO2) and Water (H2O).",
            "The arrow points to what happens when sunlight hits chlorophyll—energy drives the reaction!",
            "On the right are the outputs: Glucose (C6H12O6), which is sugar/food for the plant, and Oxygen (O2) released into the air.",
            "Notice that we need 6 molecules of CO2 and Water to make one molecule of glucose. It's a precise balance!",
            "This equation shows why plants are vital: they turn waste gas (CO2) into food and oxygen for us.",
            "Time estimate: 5 minutes."
          ],
          "visualAttribution": {
            "visualPlan": null,
            "svgDiagram": null
          },
          "visualPlan": "Animated flowchart showing molecules combining to form glucose and releasing oxygen bubbles.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Chemical reaction visualization",
              "searchQuery": "photosynthesis chemical equation animation science education",
              "sourceSite": "Wikimedia Commons",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "CC BY-SA 4.0",
              "attributionText": "Diagram by Science Education Resources (Public Domain)",
              "altText": "Visual representation of the photosynthesis chemical equation.",
              "placementHint": "Center stage, large text."
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": {
            "title": "",
            "type": "",
            "instructions": [],
            "svgCode": ""
          },
          "animationHints": [
            "Animate molecules moving from left to right",
            "Highlight 'Sunlight' above the arrow"
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 5
        },
        {
          "templateId": "visual-focus",
          "templateSlideKey": "guided_practice",
          "templateSlideTitle": "Activity: The Leaf Detective",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 9,
          "slideType": "guided_practice",
          "slideTitle": "Class Activity: Identify the Parts",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO-001",
            "LO-003"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Application of Concepts through Observation"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "Engage students in active learning by identifying parts of a leaf and their functions.",
          "studentTakeaway": "Students can identify stomata, chlorophyll, and veins on a real or diagrammed leaf.",
          "layout": "activity-card",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "Look at the provided leaf sample/diagram",
            "Identify: Stomata (pores), Veins (water transport)",
            "Discuss: Where does sunlight get absorbed?"
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "Task 1: Find the stomata!",
            "Task 2: Trace the water path.",
            "Discussion: Why are leaves green?",
            "Time limit: 5 minutes"
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Now it's your turn to be scientists! Look at this leaf (or diagram).",
            "Your first task is to find the stomata. Remember, these are tiny pores for breathing.",
            "Second, trace where water travels from roots up through the veins.",
            "Finally, discuss with your partner: Why do you think leaves are green? What would happen if they were black?",
            "Walk around and help students connect the visual parts to our earlier definitions of chlorophyll and stomata.",
            "Time estimate: 5 minutes."
          ],
          "visualAttribution": {
            "visualPlan": null,
            "svgDiagram": null
          },
          "visualPlan": "High-resolution image of a leaf with callout lines pointing to specific features (stomata, veins).",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Interactive activity visual aid",
              "searchQuery": "leaf anatomy diagram labeled stomata veins biology education",
              "sourceSite": "Khan Academy Visuals",
              "sourceUrl": "",
              "licenseType": "CC BY 4.0",
              "attributionText": "Diagram by Biology Education Project (Public Domain)",
              "altText": "Labeled diagram of a leaf showing stomata and veins.",
              "placementHint": "Center stage."
            }
          ],
          "svgDiagram": {
            "title": "",
            "type": "",
            "instructions": [],
            "svgCode": ""
          },
          "animationHints": [
            "Reveal labels one by one as students identify them"
          ],
          "timeEstimateMinutes": 5
        },
        {
          "templateId": "visual-focus",
          "templateSlideKey": "quick_assessment",
          "templateSlideTitle": "Check Your Understanding",
          "isOptionalSlotFilled": true,
          "slideNumber": 10,
          "slideType": "quick_assessment",
          "slideTitle": "Quick Quiz: Photosynthesis Check",
          "learningOutcomeIds": [
            "LO-002"
          ],
          "topicCoverage": [
            "Formative Assessment of Learning Outcomes"
          ],
          "teacherIntent": "Assess student understanding through targeted questions.",
          "studentTakeaway": "Students can answer key questions about reactants and products of photosynthesis.",
          "layout": "quiz-card",
          "bulletPoints": [
            "1. What gas do plants take in?",
            "2. Where does the energy come from?",
            "3. Name one product made by plants."
          ],
          "onSlideText": [
            "Q1: Input Gas? (CO2)",
            "Q2: Energy Source? (Sunlight)",
            "Q3: Product Made? (Glucose/Oxygen)"
          ],
          "speakerNotes": [
            "Let's check what we've learned. Raise your hand for each answer.",
            "Question 1: What gas do plants take in from the air to make food? That's right, Carbon Dioxide!",
            "Question 2: Where does the energy come from to drive this reaction? Sunlight is key here.",
            "Question 3: Name one thing a plant makes. You can say Glucose (food) or Oxygen (air).",
            "If you got these right, you are ready for homework! If not, review the formula on Slide 8.",
            "Time estimate: 4 minutes."
          ],
          "visualAttribution": {
            "visualPlan": null,
            "svgDiagram": null
          },
          "visualPlan": "Simple quiz interface with question bubbles and checkmarks for correct answers.",
          "assets": [
            {
              "purpose": "Visual reinforcement of assessment",
              "searchQuery": "science quiz icons checkmark bubble education",
              "sourceSite": "Unsplash Education Collection",
